blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, All-wise. (2)
إِنَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
In the heavens and the earth there are indeed signs for the faithful, (3)
وَفِى خَلْقِكُمْ وَمَا يَبُثُّ مِن دَآبَّةٍ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
There are signs for people who have certainty in your own creation and in animals, He scatters abroad. (4)
وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن رِّزْقٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٥)
There are signs for people who exercise their reason in the alternation of night and day, in the provision that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death, and in the changing of the winds. (5)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
These are the signs of Allah that We recite for you in truth. So what discourse will they believe after Allah and His signs? (6)
وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ (٧)
Woe to every sinful liar, (7)
يَسْمَعُ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٨)
who hears the signs of Allah being recited to him, yet persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them. So inform him of a painful punishment. (8)
وَإِذَا عَلِمَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا شَيْـًٔا ٱتَّخَذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٩)
Should he learn anything about Our signs, he takes them in derision. For such there is a humiliating punishment. (9)
مِّن وَرَآئِهِمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مَّا كَسَبُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا مَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠)
Ahead of them is hell, and neither what they have earned, nor what they had taken as guardians besides Allah will avail them in any way, and there is a great punishment for them. (10)
هَـٰذَا هُدًى ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ (١١)
This is [true] guidance, and there is a painful punishment due to defilement for those who defy the signs of their Lord. (11)
۞ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢)
It is Allah who disposed the sea for you[r benefit] so that the ships may sail in it by His command, that you may seek of His bounty and that you may give thanks. (12)
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١٣)
He has disposed for you[r benefit] whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth; all is from Him. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (13)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is a general call based on warning (الإنذار). It opens with verses on monotheism (الوحدانية) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — then mentions the legislation of the law (تشريع الشريعة) for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. It points to the necessity of following it for him and for others, given that before them lies a day in which they will be held accountable for their righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) — the virtuous actions performed with faith and in accordance with divine command — such as faith (الإيمان) and following the law (الشريعة), and for their commission of evil deeds (اجتراح السيئات) by turning away from the religion (الدين). It then mentions what will happen to both groups on that day, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة).

Within its objectives is a warning and a severe threat to the arrogant (المستكبرين) who turn away from the signs (آيات) of Allah, and those who have taken their own desire (هواهم) as their god, and whom Allah has led astray despite [their] knowledge.

Among its unique topics is the explanation of the meaning of the recording of deeds (كتابة الأعمال) and their transcription (استنساخها).

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as evidenced by the context of its verses. Some have excepted His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — "Say to those who have faith" (45:14), but there is no evidence for this exception.

His saying, the Exalted: حم تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ — "Ha, Meem. The sent-down [revelation] of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (45:1-2). It appears that "the sent-down [revelation] of the Book" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ) is an instance of appending the attribute to the described object, where the verbal noun (المصدر) carries the meaning of the passive participle (المفعول). The phrase "from Allah" (مِنَ اللهِ) relates to "sent-down" (تنزيل), and the entire phrase is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدإ).

The meaning is: This is a Book sent down from Allah, the All-mighty (العزيز), the All-wise (الحكيم). The discussion regarding the individual words of the verse has already been presented previously.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "In the heavens and the earth there are indeed signs for the faithful" (45:3). The sign (آية) of a thing is its mark (علامته) that points to it and indicates it. The meaning of the heavens and the earth containing signs is that they are, in themselves, signs for Him. There is nothing beyond the heavens, the earth, and the rest of what Allah has created that serves as a container for them such that it would be a sign pointing to Him, the Exalted.

Evidence for what we have mentioned is the variation in expression regarding this in His speech, the Exalted. Sometimes He mentions that "in" the thing there is a sign for Him, and at other times He considers the thing itself to be a sign, such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَاخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ لَآياتٍ — "Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day there are signs..." (3:190), and His saying: وَمِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth..." (30:22). There are many similar examples. It is understood from the variation of expression therein that the meaning of a thing having a sign "in" it is that it is itself a sign. This is also understood from the variation in expressions like His saying: "Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day there are signs," and His saying: "In the heavens and the earth there are indeed signs," that what is meant by the creation of the heavens and the earth is the heavens and the earth themselves and nothing else.

The rhetorical purpose (العناية) in taking the thing as a container (ظرفا) for the sign, despite it being a sign itself, is to consider the aspects (جهات) of its existence; for its existence has an aspect or aspects, each of which is one of the signs (الآيات). If the thing itself were taken [as the sign], it would only be correct to take it as a single sign, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي الْأَرْضِ آياتٌ لِلْمُوقِنِينَ — "And in the earth are signs for those who have certainty" (51:20). If the sign were taken to be the earth itself, it would only be correct to say: "And the earth is a sign for those who have certainty," and the intended meaning would be lost—which is that in the existence of the earth there are aspects, each of which is a sign on its own.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: "In the heavens and the earth," etc., is that the existence of the heavens and the earth has aspects indicating that Allah, the Exalted, is their Creator and their Governor (المدبر) alone, without any partner. For in their essential need (حاجتها الذاتية) for one who brings them into existence, the greatness of their creation, the ingenuity of their composition, the continuity of the existence of their parts with one another, and their interconnectedness despite their immense multiplicity—and the inclusion of their specific particular systems (أنظمتها الجزئية) under a general system (نظام عام) that encompasses and governs them—all indicate that they have a Creator who is alone their Lord (ربها) who governs their affairs. Were there not one who brings them into existence, they would not have existed at all; and were their Governor not one, the systems would have conflicted, clashed, and the governance would have differed.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the statement of some—that His saying: "In the heavens" (فِي السَّماواتِ) implies a suppressed prefixed noun, the estimation being "in the creation of the heavens"—is an unnecessary affectation (تكلف) for which there is no compelling need.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي خَلْقِكُمْ وَما يَبُثُّ مِنْ دابَّةٍ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — "And in your creation and in the animals He scatters, there are signs for a people who have certainty" (45:4). Scattering (البث) means dispersing and stirring up. His scattering of animals is His creating them, dispersing them, and spreading them across the earth, just as He said regarding the creation of man: ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ — "...then, behold, you are humans scattering [everywhere]" (30:20).

The meaning of the verse is: And in yourselves, in terms of your created existence, and in the animals Allah disperses, in terms of their creation, there are signs for a people who tread the path of certainty (اليقين) — the firm and unwavering conviction in the truth that is free from any doubt.

The creation of man, as an earthly being connected to matter (المادة), is another type of creation that differs from the creation of the heavens and the earth. This is because he is composed of an earthly body (بدن أرضي) made of elemental cosmic materials (مواد كونية عنصرية) that perish upon death through dispersal and disintegration, and another matter beyond that, celestial (علوي) and non-material (غير مادي), which does not perish upon death but is rather taken away (يتوفى) and preserved with Allah. This is what the Quran calls the spirit (الروح). The Exalted said: وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي — "...and breathed into him of My spirit" (15:29). And He said, after mentioning the creation of man from a drop, then a clinging mass, then a fleshy tissue, then completing the creation of his body: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ — "...then We produced him as another creation" (23:14). And He said: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls...’" (32:11).

Thus, one who observes the creation of man observes a sign of the celestial realm (ملكوتية) beyond the material signs. Likewise, one who observes the creation of animals, for they possess souls (نفوس) endowed with life and consciousness (شعور), even if they are below man in their life and consciousness, just as they are below him in their physical equipment. In all of these are signs for the people of certainty, through which they recognize Allah, the Glorified, as being One, with no partner in His Lordship (ربوبيته) or His Divinity (ألوهيته).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ — "And the alternation of night and day..." (45:5) until the end of the verse. This category of signs consists of the signs between the heaven and the earth.

By His saying: "And the alternation of night and day," He means their variation in length and shortness—a variation organized by the change of the four seasons according to the different regions of the earth, repeating with the repetition of the years. Through this, the Glorified provides the sustenance (أقوات) for the inhabitants of the earth and nurtures them with a proper nurturing. The Exalted said: وَقَدَّرَ فِيها أَقْواتَها فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَواءً لِلسَّائِلِينَ — "...and ordained its provisions in four days, alike for the seekers" (41:10).

His saying: وَما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بعد مَوْتِها — "...and the provision that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death..." (45:5). What is meant by the "provision" (الرزق) that Allah sends down from the sky is rain—naming the cause by the name of the effect metaphorically (مجازا), or because rain is also part of the provision, as the waters of the earth come from rain. "The sky" (السماء) refers to the direction of height or the clouds metaphorically. Reviving the earth with it after its death is the revival of the plants within it by their starting to grow and flourish. The mention of revival after death is not without an allusion to the Resurrection (المعاد).

His saying: وَتَصْرِيفِ الرِّياحِ — "...and the shifting of the winds..." (45:5) means their turning and sending from one side to another. Their shifting has many general benefits, the most common of which are driving the clouds to the regions of the earth, pollinating plants, and dispelling foulness and stinking odors.

His saying: آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ — "...are signs for a people who exercise their reason" (45:5), meaning those who distinguish between truth and falsehood, and the beautiful and the ugly, using the intellect (العقل) that Allah, the Glorified, has deposited within them.

Each category of signs has been specified for a particular group: the sign of the heavens and the earth was specified for the believers (المؤمنين); the sign of man and other animals for a people who have certainty (يوقنون); and the sign of the alternation of night and day, the rains, and the shifting of the winds for a people who exercise their reason (يعقلون).

Perhaps the reason for this is that the sign of the heavens and the earth indicates, by a simple and straightforward (ساذجة) indication, that they did not bring themselves into existence, nor did they arise by chance or coincidence. Rather, they have an Originator who brought them into existence along with their properties and actions from which the observed system results. Their Creator is the Creator of all and the Lord of everything. Man perceives this with his simple, straightforward understanding, and the believers of all levels understand this and benefit from it.

As for His creation of man and other animals that possess life and consciousness, they—in terms of their living and conscious spirits and souls—belong to a world beyond the world of matter, which is called the celestial realm (الملكوت). The Quran has reserved the perfection of its perception and witnessing for the people of certainty (أهل اليقين), as He said: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who have certainty" (6:75).

As for the sign of the alternation of night and day, the rains that revive the earth, and the shifting of the winds, because of the diversity of their types, the multiplicity of their aspects, their connection to the earth and earthly things, and the abundance of their benefits and the breadth of their utilities, they require detailed and deep intellectual reflection (تعقل فكري). They are not attained through simple, straightforward understanding. Therefore, they were specified for a people who exercise their reason (يعقلون). The signs are signs for all people, but since those who benefit from them are only some of them, they were specified for them.

The people of certainty and reason were referred to with the verbal forms "a people who have certainty" (قوم يوقنون) and "a people who exercise their reason" (قوم يعقلون), while the people of faith were referred to as "the believers" (المؤمنين). This is because the simplicity of the sign for the people of faith implies that what is meant by faith is its essence, which is stable within them; thus, it was appropriate to refer to them by the descriptive noun (الوصف). This is in contrast to the signs for the people of certainty and reason, which, due to their subtlety and the height of their attainment, are perceived bit by bit; thus, they were appropriate for the present-tense verb (الفعل المضارع) which indicates continuous renewal (الاستمرار التجددي).

Regarding the order of the three verses and their respective people—where He first mentioned the people of faith, then certainty, then reason—it has been said that this follows the order of progression (الترقي). For certainty (الإيقان) is a specific degree within faith, so it comes after faith. Reason (العقل) is the pivot of faith and certainty—meaning the reason aided by the light of insight—for through it, certainty is purified from the onset of doubts in every respect, and in its strengthening lies all good. The order of the verses followed the order of these three degrees. (1)

(1) This view is derived from al-Kashshaf; what follows it is by the author of al-Kashf, and the final view is by al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir.

However, the implication of what he described regarding the matter of reason is that it should occur before the second, or even before the first of the degrees. Furthermore, what he mentioned regarding the possibility of doubts encroaching upon certainty is something that cannot be conceived.

It has also been said regarding the order: that the completion of the observation in the second necessitates observing the first, because the heavens and the earth are among the causes for the formation of animals in a sense, so they must be mentioned before them. Likewise, the observation in the third necessitates observing the first two: as for the first, it is obvious; as for the second, it is because it is the final cause (العلة الغائية), so it must be comprehensive—meaning that the third, which is the effect (المعلول), depends for its recognition on the mention of its final cause before it.

The critique of this is that, even if it were correct, it would be a reason for the order of the signs, not the degrees of the three attributes—namely faith, certainty, and reason. Furthermore, the third is also, like the first, among the causes for the formation of animals, so it should precede the second. From another perspective, the second is the final cause for the first, so it should precede the first just as it preceded the third.

It has also been said: The reason for the order of these closing phrases (الفواصل) is that it was as if it were said: If you are believers, then understand these proofs; and if you are not believers but are seekers of conviction and certainty, then understand these proofs; and if you are neither believers nor possessors of certainty, then strive in the recognition of these proofs.

The critique of this is that, even if it were correct, it would be a reason for the order of the three attributes, not the three types of signs. Moreover, its implication is that none of the three signs would be specific to one of the three attributes; rather, all would be for all, and the context does not support this. Furthermore, the apparent meaning of his words is that he interpreted certainty (اليقين) as conviction (الجزم), which is knowledge (العلم); thus, nothing remains for reason (العقل) except conjectural judgment (الحكم الظني), which is not given weight in the sciences of belief (المعارف الاعتقادية).

His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ آياتُ اللهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِّ فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَ اللهِ وَآياتِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "These are the signs of Allah that We recite to you truly. So in what discourse will they believe after Allah and His signs?" (45:6). Faith (الإيمان) in a matter is the knowledge of it along with a commitment to it in practice. If there is no commitment, it is not faith, even if knowledge exists. The Exalted said: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts..." (27:14). And He said: وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ — "...and whom Allah has led astray despite [his] knowledge..." (45:23).

The signs (الآيات) are the indicating marks. The cosmic signs (الآيات الكونية) of Allah are the cosmic matters that indicate, by their external existence, that He, the Exalted, is One in creation, described with attributes of perfection, and transcendent above every deficiency and need. Faith in these signs is faith in their indication of Him, the Exalted, and its necessity is faith in Him, the Exalted, as they indicate Him.

The Quranic signs (الآيات القرآنية) are signs for Him, the Exalted, in what they indicate of the cosmic signs that point to Him, the Glorified, or regarding the sciences of belief, practical rulings (أحكام عملية), or ethics (أخلاق) that Allah, the Glorified, approves of and commands. For their contents indicate Him and are from Him. Faith in these signs is also faith in their indication, and its necessity is faith in what they indicate.

The miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) are also either cosmic signs—and their indication is like that of cosmic signs—or non-cosmic, like the Quran in its miraculousness, and the reference of their indication is to the indication of cosmic signs.

His saying: "These are the signs of Allah that We recite to you" (تِلْكَ آياتُ اللهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ) refers to the Quranic signs recited to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. It is also possible that it refers to the cosmic signs mentioned in the three preceding verses, by virtue of the unity between the indicator and the indicated.

His saying: "So in what discourse will they believe after Allah and His signs?" (فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَ اللهِ وَآياتِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ). It has been said: This is like your saying, "Zayd and his generosity impressed me," when in fact only his generosity impressed you. The meaning, according to precise observation, is: "The generosity of Zayd impressed me, and Zayd from the aspect of his generosity." Thus, the meaning of the verse is: In what discourse after the signs of Allah—meaning the Quranic signs—will they believe? That is, if they do not believe in this discourse, in what discourse after it will they believe?

It has also been said: The speech implies a suppressed "discourse" (حديث); that is, if they do not believe, then in what discourse after the discourse of Allah and His signs will they believe? It is more appropriate, according to this meaning, that the "signs" refer to the cosmic signs. Therefore, al-Tabarsi said after mentioning this meaning: "The difference between the discourse (الحديث), which is the Quran, and the signs (الآيات) is that the discourse consists of stories from which lessons are extracted to clarify truth from falsehood, while the signs are the decisive proofs between the sound and the corrupt." End quote. The first of the two views is more elegant.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ — "Woe to every sinful liar!" (45:7). Woe (الويل) is destruction. The liar (الأفاك) is an intensive form of lying (الإفك). The sinful (الأثيم) is from sin (الإثم), meaning disobedience. The meaning is: Let destruction be upon every liar who is a possessor of sin.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْمَعُ آياتُ اللهِ تُتْلى عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها — "who hears the signs of Allah being recited to him, then persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them..." (45:8) until the end of the verse. This is a description of every sinful liar. "Then" (ثُمَ) indicates a rank-based delay (التراخي الرتبي) and conveys the sense of improbability (الاستبعاد). Persistence (الإصرار) in an act is to adhere to it and not detach from it.

The meaning is: He hears the signs of Allah—which are the verses of the Quran—being read to him, then he adheres to disbelief while being arrogant, not humbling himself to the truth, as if he had not heard those signs. So give him the news of a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا عَلِمَ مِنْ آياتِنا شَيْئاً اتَّخَذَها هُزُواً — "And when he comes to know anything of Our signs, he takes them in derision..." (45:9) until the end of the verse. The context suggests that the pronoun in "takes them" (اتخذها) refers to the signs. Making the derision relate to the signs [as a whole] rather than just what he came to know of them indicates the completeness of his ignorance. The meaning is: When that sinful, persisting, arrogant liar comes to know some of Our signs, he mocks all of Our signs.

His saying: أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ — "For such there is a humiliating punishment" (45:9), meaning debasing and disgraceful. Describing the punishment as humiliating (مهين) is in contrast to their arrogance and derision. The reference "such" (أولئك) is to every liar. Other views have been mentioned regarding the verse, which we have set aside due to their lack of benefit.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَلا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ ما كَسَبُوا شَيْئاً وَلا مَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْلِياءَ — "Ahead of them is hell, and what they have earned will not avail them in any way, nor those whom they took as guardians besides Allah..." (45:10) until the end of the verse. Since they were occupied with the world, turning away from the truth, and unmindful of the consequences of their deeds, Hell was placed "behind them" (وراءهم) even though it is in front of them and they are traveling toward it and facing it.

It has been said: "Behind them" (وراءهم) means in front of them. It is said in al-Majma': "Wara' (وراء) is a noun that applies to both the front and the back; whatever is hidden from you is wara' to you, whether it is behind you or in front of you." End quote. In His saying: "Ahead of them is hell," there is a final decree.

His saying: "and what they have earned will not avail them in any way" (وَلا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ ما كَسَبُوا شَيْئاً). What is meant by "what they have earned" is what they acquired in the world of wealth and the like. The indefiniteness of "anything" (شَيْئاً) is for belittlement (التحقير); that is, what they earned of wealth, status, and supporters in the world will not avail them in the least on the Day of Reckoning.

His saying: "nor those whom they took as guardians besides Allah" (وَلا مَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْلِياءَ). The "ma" (ما) is a gerundive particle (مصدرية). What is meant by "guardians" (الأولياء) are the masters of the idols whom they took as lords and gods, claiming they were intercessors for them, or the idols themselves.

His saying: وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ — "...and there is a great punishment for them" (45:10) is a confirmation of the threat against them. Allah, the Glorified, threatened them first with His saying: "Woe to every sinful liar," etc.; second with His saying: "So give him the news of a painful punishment"; third with His saying: "For such there is a humiliating punishment"; fourth with His saying: "Ahead of them is hell," etc.; and fifth with His saying: "and there is a great punishment for them." He described their punishment throughout these as being painful (أليم), humiliating (مهين), and great (عظيم).

His saying, the Exalted: هذا هُدىً وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مِنْ رِجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ — "This is a guidance; and those who defy the signs of their Lord, for them there is a painful punishment of a [foul] plague" (45:11).

The reference in His saying: "This is a guidance" (هذا هُدىً) is to the Quran. Describing it as "guidance" is for emphasis (المبالغة), like saying "Zayd is justice." The plague (الرجز) — as has been said — is the severest of punishment, and its root meaning is agitation (الاضطراب).

The verse is in the position of refuting what they cast upon the Quran, considering it contemptible through derision and mockery, and it is a summary of the threat against those who disbelieve in His signs.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْبَحْرَ لِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِ — "It is Allah who has disposed the sea for you that the ships may sail on it by His command..." (45:12) until the end of the verse. When the Glorified mentioned the state of the liars—their arrogance against believing in the signs when they are recited to them, and their mockery of what they knew of them—and threatened them with the most eloquent threat of the severest punishment, He returned to them with a discourse addressed to all, both those who believe and those who disbelieve. He mentioned some of the signs of His Lordship (ربوبيته) which contain great blessings for them and which they cannot deny. So He first mentioned the disposal of the sea for them, then what is in the heavens and the earth entirely. In these are signs that none disbelieve in except one who has stripped himself of human primordial nature (الفطرة) and forgotten reflection (التفكر), which is one of the clearest characteristics of man.

So His saying: "It is Allah who has disposed the sea for you" (اللهُ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْبَحْرَ). The "lam" (لـ) in "for you" (لَكُمُ) is for purpose (للغاية); that is, He disposed the sea for your sake by creating it in a way that it carries ships and allows them to sail in it, so that man may benefit from it. It is also possible that it is for transition (للتعدية), meaning that man disposes the sea by the permission of Allah.

His saying: "that the ships may sail on it by His command" (لِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِ) is the purpose for the disposal of the sea. The sailing of the ships in it by His command is the bringing forth of the sailing by the word "Be" (كن). Thus, the effects of things, like the things themselves, are attributed to Him, the Exalted. His saying: وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "...and that you may seek His grace" (45:12), meaning that you may seek His gift through boarding it, which is His provision (رزقه).

His saying: وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "...and so that you may give thanks" (45:12), meaning in the hope that you will thank Him, the Exalted, for this blessing, which is the disposal of the sea.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَخَّرَ لكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً مِنْهُ — "He has disposed for you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth; all is from Him..." (45:13) until the end of the verse. This is an instance of progression (الترقي) by appending the general to the specific. The discussion regarding "for you" (لَكُمْ) is like the discussion regarding its equivalent in the previous verse. His saying: "all" (جَمِيعاً) is an emphasis for "whatever is in the heavens and the earth," or a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for it.

His saying: "He has disposed for you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth; all" (سَخَّرَ لكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً). The meaning of their disposal for man is that the parts of the observed world proceed according to a single system that governs them, links them to one another, and links the whole to man. Thus, he benefits in his life from its celestial and terrestrial parts. Human society continues to expand its utilization of them, benefiting from their mediation and employing their various elements to obtain the advantages of life. Thus, everything is disposed for him.

His saying: "from Him" (مِنْهُ). The "min" (من) is for origin (للابتداء), and the pronoun refers to Allah, the Exalted. It is a circumstantial qualifier for what is in the heavens and the earth. The meaning is: He disposed for you whatever is in the heavens and the earth entirely, while it originates from Him and is obtained from Him. For the essences (ذوات) of things originate from Him through His bringing them into existence without a prior model, and likewise their properties and effects. Among their properties and effects is the interconnectedness of some with others, which is the system (النظام) proceeding within them that is linked to man. The Exalted said: اللهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ — "Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back..." (30:11). And He said: إِنَّهُ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ — "Indeed it is He who originates and brings back" (85:13).

They have mentioned other meanings for His saying: "from Him" (مِنْهُ), none of which are free from affectation (التكلف), so we have omitted mentioning them.

His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect" (45:13). The reason for its connection to reflection (التفكر) is clear.

Ayahs

قُل لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ ٱللَّهِ لِيَجْزِىَ قَوْمًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٤)
Say to the faithful to forgive those who do not expect Allah’s days, that He may [Himself] requite every people for what they used to earn. (14)
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٥)
Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment, then you will be brought back to your Lord. (15)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things. We gave them an advantage over all the nations, (16)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٧)
and We gave them manifest precepts. But they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (17)
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ فَٱتَّبِعْهَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨)
Then We set you upon a clear course of the Law; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know. (18)
إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا۟ عَنكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩)
Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah. Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the Godwary. (19)

(Commentary)

After mentioning the verses of divine unity (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and alluding therein to the Resurrection (المعاد) and Prophethood (النبوة) within the context of the revelation of the Book and the warning to the arrogant who mock it, He mentions in these verses the legislation of the Law (الشريعة) for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He approaches this through two premises that link it to the preceding discourse. The first is the call to the believers to refrain from confronting the disbelievers (الكفار) who do not expect the "Days of Allah" (أيام الله), for Allah will requite them, as all deeds—whether righteous or evil—are subject to accountability. This is the reason for the legislation of the Law. The second premise is that the sending down of the Book, Authority (الحكمة), and Prophethood is not an unprecedented innovation (بدع); indeed, Allah gave the Children of Israel the Book, Authority, and Prophethood, and granted them clear proofs (البينات) such that no doubt could remain for any doubter regarding the religion of Allah, except that their scholars differed therein out of envy (بغيا) among themselves, and Allah will judge between them.

Then, the Glorified mentions the legislation of the Law for him, commanding him to follow it and forbidding him from following the whims (أهواء) of the ignorant.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا لِلَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللهِ "Say to those who have faith that they should forgive those who do not expect the days of Allah" (45:14) etc. This is a command from the Exalted to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to command the believers to forgive the disbelievers. Thus, the implicit structure of the verse is: "Say to them: Forgive, and they shall forgive." This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِعِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ "Say to My servants who have faith that they should maintain the prayer" (14:31).

The verse is Meccan, situated within the context of the preceding verses describing the state of the arrogant who mock the signs of Allah and threatening them with the severest punishment. It appears that when the believers in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw these mockers persisting in their slander and insults against the Prophet and their mockery of Allah's signs, they could not restrain themselves from defending the Book of Allah and the one sent with it, and from calling them to renounce their ways and believe—even though they were among those against whom the word of punishment had become due, as is apparent from the previous verses. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to order them to pardon and overlook them, and not to concern themselves with their state, for the evil consequences of their deeds will surely overtake them, and the recompense for what they have earned will reach them.

Accordingly, the meaning of "forgiveness" (المغفرة) in His saying: قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا "Say to those who have faith that they should forgive" is to overlook and turn away from them by ceasing to quarrel or argue with them. The meaning of الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللهِ "those who do not expect the days of Allah" refers to those mentioned in the previous verses; they do not anticipate any "Days of Allah" wherein no judgment or sovereignty belongs to anyone but Allah, the Exalted—such as the day of death, the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection, the Day of Resurrection, and the day of exterminating punishment.

His saying: لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ "so that He may [one day] reward a people for what they used to earn" (45:14) is the rationale for the command to forgive, or for the command to issue the command to forgive. The gist is: let them overlook them and not confront them, for there is no need for that, as Allah will requite them for what they used to earn. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: وَذَرْنِي وَالْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلاً إِنَّ لَدَيْنا أَنْكالاً وَجَحِيماً "Leave Me to deal with the deniers, the possessors of ease and plenty, and give them a little respite. Indeed with Us are heavy fetters and a fierce fire" (73:11-12), and His saying: ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ "then leave them to play around in their impious gossip" (6:91), and His saying: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ "So leave them to gossip and play until they encounter their day which they are promised" (70:42), and His saying: فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ "So pardon them and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know" (43:89).

The meaning of the verse is: Command those who believe to pardon and overlook those arrogant ones who mock the signs of Allah and do not anticipate the Days of Allah, so that Allah may requite them for what they used to earn—and the Day of Recompense is one of His Days. That is, let them overlook these deniers of the Days of Allah until He requites them for their deeds on one of His Days.

In His saying: لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً "so that He may reward a people," a manifest noun is used in place of a pronoun. The apparent requirement was to say: "so that He may reward them." The subtle point (النكتة) in this—along with the word "people" (قوماً) being an indefinite noun without an adjective—is to belittle their matter and show a lack of concern for them, as if they are an unknown group whose identity is unrecognized and whose affairs are of no importance.

Through the aforementioned explanation of the verse's meaning, this verse and what follows it connect to what preceded it, and the objections raised against it—which others have struggled to answer—are dispelled. Furthermore, the corruption of the various meanings they have mentioned for it becomes clear; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the more extensive commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ "Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment; then you will be brought back to your Lord" (45:15). This stands as the rationale for His saying: لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً "so that He may reward a people" etc., which is why it is not joined by a conjunction and is not a new beginning (استئناف) at all.

The sum of the meaning is: Allah will requite them for what they earned, for deeds do not vanish in vain or without effect; rather, whoever performs a righteous deed (العمل الصالح) benefits from it, and whoever does an evil deed is harmed by it. Then to your Lord you shall return, and He will requite you according to your deeds: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood" (45:16) etc. Having explained that deeds have good or bad effects that attach to their doers, He intended to draw attention to the legislation of a Law for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as it is incumbent upon Allah, the Glorified, to guide His servants to that which contains their goodness and felicity, as He, the Exalted, said: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ "With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path, and some of them are devious" (16:9).

He drew attention to this in His upcoming saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكَ عَلى شَرِيعَةٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ "Then We set you on a course of the Law" (45:18) etc. He preceded this by referring to what He gave the Children of Israel of the Book, Authority, and Prophethood, and His providing them with good things, His favoring them, and His giving them clear proofs. This was to announce that the divine outpouring (الإفاضة) of the Law, Prophethood, and the Book is not an unprecedented innovation without prior example; rather, it has a parallel in the Children of Israel, who are within their sight and hearing.

So, in His saying: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ, the "Book" (الكتاب) refers to the Torah, which contains the Law of Moses عليه‌السلام. As for the Gospel (الإنجيل), it does not contain a Law; its Law is the Law of the Torah. As for the Psalms (زبور) of David, they consist of supplications and litanies. It is possible that "the Book" refers to the genus that includes the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms, as has been suggested, but this is made unlikely by the fact that "the Book" is not used in the Qur'an except for that which contains a Law.

The "Authority" (الحكمة), by the context of its mention alongside the Book, refers to that which the Book judges and decrees regarding the duties of the people, as mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ "and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, so that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213). He said regarding the Torah: يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَالْأَحْبارُ بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ "by which the prophets who had submitted [to Allah] would judge for the Jews, and [so would] the priests and the scribes, as they were entrusted with the Book of Allah" (5:44). Thus, Authority is an inseparable concomitant of the Book, just as Prophethood is an inseparable concomitant of it.

The meaning of "Prophethood" (النبوة) is well-known; Allah raised a vast multitude of prophets from the Children of Israel, as stated in the narrations, and He recounted a group of their messengers in His Book.

His saying: وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ "and We provided them with all the good things" (45:16) means wholesome provisions, among which were manna and quails.

His saying: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ "and We gave them an advantage over all the nations" (45:16). If the meaning is all the worlds (العالمين) — all creation across all times, then they were favored in certain respects, such as the abundance of prophets sent to them and the many manifest miracles performed by their prophets. If the meaning is the people of their own time, then they were favored in all respects.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْناهُمْ بَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ "and We gave them manifest proofs of the Law" (45:17) to the end of the verse. The "clear proofs" (البينات) refer to the manifest signs that remove every doubt and uncertainty and erase it from the truth. This is evidenced by the subsequent clause: فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ "and they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them".

Regarding "the Law" (الأمر), it is said to be the matter of religion (الدين), and the preposition "from" (من) means "in." The meaning would be: "We gave them clear proofs in the matter of religion," which includes the miracles of Moses عليه‌السلام.

It is also said that it refers to the matter of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning would be: "We gave them signs regarding the matter of the Prophet and clear marks of his truthfulness," such as his appearance in Mecca, his migration from there to Yathrib, the support of his people, and other things mentioned in their books.

His saying: فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ "and they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (45:17) indicates that the differences that appeared among them in religion and the mixing of falsehood with truth did not arise from ambiguity or ignorance; rather, their scholars created them out of envy, and envy was prevalent among them.

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ "Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ" (45:17) is an indication that their differing—which is not free from the mixing of falsehood with truth—does not vanish in vain; it will surely have its effect, and Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection, requiting them according to what their deeds necessitate.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكَ عَلى شَرِيعَةٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ فَاتَّبِعْها وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "Then We set you on a course of the Law; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know" (45:18). The address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and his community (الأمة) shares in it. The "Law" (الشريعة) literally means a path leading to water, and "the matter" (الأمر) refers to the matter of religion. The meaning is: after We gave the Children of Israel what We gave them, We set you upon a specific path of the divine religion, which is the Islamic Law that Allah has specifically assigned to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his community.

His saying: فَاتَّبِعْها "so follow it" etc., is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to follow what is revealed to him of the religion and not to follow the whims of the ignorant that oppose the divine religion.

From the verse, it appears, firstly: that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is legally obligated (مكلف) by the religion just like the rest of the community.

Secondly: that every practical ruling (حكم عملي) that is not based on divine revelation (الوحي) and does not originate from it is merely one of the whims of the ignorant, unrelated to knowledge (العلم).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُمْ لَنْ يُغْنُوا عَنْكَ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً "Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah" (45:19) etc. This is the rationale for the prohibition against following the whims of those who do not know. "Availing" (الإغناء) against something means removing the need for it. The gist is: you have essential needs before Allah, the Glorified, which none but He can fulfill, and the means to that is following His religion and nothing else. Therefore, these people whose whims you might follow cannot fulfill any of your needs for you, nor can they provide any degree of availment.

His saying: وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ "Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the God-conscious" (45:19). What the context provides is that this is another rationale for the prohibition against following the whims of the ignorant. The "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) — the unjust who overstep the bounds of truth — are those who follow their invented whims, and the "God-conscious" (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى) and protect themselves from Allah's displeasure — are those who follow the religion of Allah.

The meaning is: Allah is the Guardian (الولي) of those who follow His religion because they are God-conscious, and Allah is their Guardian. As for those who follow the whims of the ignorant, He, the Exalted, is not their Guardian; rather, they are guardians of one another because they are wrongdoers, and the wrongdoers are guardians of one another. Therefore, follow the religion of Allah so that He may be your Guardian, and do not follow their whims, lest they become your guardians, for they will not avail you in any way against Allah.

Naming those who follow other than the religion of Allah as "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) is consistent with what is understood from His saying: أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ "‘The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers! —those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter’" (7:44-45).

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا بَصَـٰٓئِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٢٠)
These are eye-openers for mankind, and guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty. (20)
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَرَحُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن نَّجْعَلَهُمْ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَوَآءً مَّحْيَاهُمْ وَمَمَاتُهُمْ ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٢١)
Do those who have perpetrated misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgement that they make! (21)
وَخَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
Allah created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, so that every soul may be requited for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (22)
أَفَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِۦ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِۦ غِشَـٰوَةً فَمَن يَهْدِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٣)
Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, set a seal upon his hearing and heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah [has consigned him to error]? Will you not then take admonition? (23)
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَآ إِلَّا ٱلدَّهْرُ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (٢٤)
They say, ‘There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing but time destroys us.’ But they do not have any knowledge of that, and they only make conjectures. (24)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مَّا كَانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٥)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument is to say, ‘Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (25)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
Say, ‘It is Allah who gives you life, then He makes you die. Then He will gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. But most people do not know.’ (26)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٢٧)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and when the Hour sets in, the falsifiers will be losers on that day. (27)
وَتَرَىٰ كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جَاثِيَةً ۚ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٨)
And you will see every nation fallen on its knees. Every nation will be summoned to its book: ‘Today you will be requited for what you used to do. (28)
هَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبُنَا يَنطِقُ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنسِخُ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٩)
This is Our book, which speaks truly against you. Indeed We used to record what you used to do.’ (29)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٣٠)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest success! (30)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣١)
But as for the faithless, [they will be asked,] ‘Were not My signs recited to you? But you were disdainful, and you were a guilty lot. (31)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا قُلْتُم مَّا نَدْرِى مَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ إِن نَّظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ (٣٢)
When it was said, ‘‘Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,’’ you said, ‘‘We do not know what the Hour is. We know nothing beyond conjectures, and we do not possess any certainty.’’ ’ (32)
وَبَدَا لَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٣)
The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (33)
وَقِيلَ ٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٣٤)
And it will be said, ‘Today We will forget you, just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours. The Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers. (34)
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّكُمُ ٱتَّخَذْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٣٥)
That is because you took the signs of Allah in derision, and the life of the world had deceived you.’ So today, they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (35)
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَرَبِّ ٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٦)
So all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth, the Lord of all the worlds. (36)
وَلَهُ ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٧)
To Him belongs all supremacy in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (37)

(Commentary)

Having alluded to placing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upon a prescribed course (شريعة) of the affair—which is the legislation of the Islamic Law—He points out in these verses that it consists of insights (بصائر) for the people, through which they perceive what they must follow of the path of the good life in this world, followed by the felicity of the life of the Hereafter; and it is a guidance and a mercy for a people who are certain of the signs of Allah.

He also points out that what drives the perpetrators of evil deeds to disdain following the Law is their denial of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and restoration of life for judgment. Thus, they imagine that they and those who follow the religion are equal in life and death, and that following the Law has no effect; hence, there is no fruit for the righteous deeds (الصالحات) to which the Law guides, except for wearying the soul with restrictions for no reason. Therefore, the Exalted demonstrates the futility of their assumption by proving the Return, then follows it with a description of the Return and the reward He bestows upon the righteous on that day, and the punishment He inflicts upon the wicked—the people of denial and crime. At that point, the Surah concludes with praise and glorification.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا بَصائِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — This is [a set of] insights for mankind, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty (45:20). The demonstration "this" (هذا) refers to the aforementioned "affair" which is the Law (الشريعة), or to the Quran insofar as it contains the Law. Insights (البصائر) is the plural of insight (بصيرة), which is the correct perception of reality (الإدراك المصيب للواقع); what is meant here is that by which one perceives. The Law is described as "insights" because it contains rulings and statutes, each of which guides to the necessary action on the path of felicity.

The meaning is: This legislated Law, or the Quran containing it, consists of practical duties through each of which people gain insight and are guided to the true path, which is the path of Allah and the path of felicity. Thus, His saying after mentioning the legislation of the Law: "This is [a set of] insights for mankind" is like His saying after mentioning the signs of Oneness at the beginning of the Surah: "This is a guidance, and those who disbelieve..." etc.

His saying: وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — and a guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty — meaning a clear direction and an outpouring of goodness for them. What is meant by "a people who have certainty" (يوقنون) are those who are certain of the signs of Allah that point to the fundamental principles of knowledge, for it is customary in the Quran for certainty (الإيقان) to be linked to the foundational beliefs.

The specification of guidance (الهدى) and mercy (الرحمة) to a people who have certainty, despite explicitly stating it is "insights for mankind," is not without support for the view that guidance here means reaching the objective (الوصول إلى المطلوب) rather than mere perception, and that mercy refers to the specific mercy (الرحمة الخاصة) for those who are God-conscious and believe in His Messenger after believing in Allah. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ — O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by, and He will forgive you (57:28). And He said: ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْغَيْبِ — This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the God-conscious, who believe in the Unseen... — until He said — وَبِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ — and are certain of the Hereafter (2:2-4). Mercy has many degrees that vary in breadth and narrowness; furthermore, the specific mercy for the people of faith also has different ranks according to the ranks of faith, for every rank of faith has a corresponding mercy.

As for mercy in the sense of absolute goodness (مطلق الخير) flowing from the Exalted, the Quran—insofar as it contains the Law—is a mercy for all mankind, just as the Messenger sent with it is a mercy for them all. The Exalted said: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ — We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds (21:107). We have provided some discussion on this meaning in previous sections.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ — Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? (45:21). It is said in al-Majma': Acquisition (الاجتراح) means earning (الاكتساب); it is said: he wounded (جرح) and he acquired (اجترح), and he earned (كسب) and he acquired (اكتسب). Its root is from "wounds" (الجراح) because such an action has an effect like the effect of wounds.

He said: An evil deed (السيئة) is an ugly act that grieves its doer due to the deserving of blame for it. [End quote].

Making (الجعل) here is in the sense of "rendering" (التصيير). The phrase "like those who have faith and do righteous deeds" (كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ) is in the position of the second object of "making," and the estimation is: "rendering them to be like those who have faith," etc.

Al-Zamakhshari asserted in al-Kashshaf that the kaf in "like those" (كَالَّذِينَ) is a noun meaning "the like of" (مثل), serving as the second object for His saying "We shall treat them" (نَجْعَلَهُمْ), and His saying "equal" (سَواءً) is a substitute (بدل) for it.

His saying "equal" (سَواءً) is in the accusative case according to the prevalent recitation, and it is an infinitive (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle, meaning "equalized" or "equivalent." His saying "their life" (مَحْياهُمْ) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) and the subject (فاعل) of "equal," and its pronoun refers to the collective of the perpetrators and the believers; "and their death" (وَمَماتُهُمْ) is coordinated with "their life" and shares its grammatical status.

The verse is phrased as a rhetorical denial, and "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة). The meaning is: Rather, do those who earn evil deeds suppose and think that We shall render them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, equal in their life and their death? That is, that the life of the former would be like the life of the latter, and their death like their death, such that faith and following the religion would be futile, having no effect in life or death, and its existence and non-existence would be the same.

His saying: ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ — Evil is the judgment they make — is a rejection of their aforementioned assumption and their judgment of equality between the perpetrators of evil and those who have faith and do righteous deeds. The "evilness" of the judgment is a metaphor for its falsehood.

Thus, the two groups are not equal in life nor in death.

As for their not being equal in life, it is because those who have faith and do righteous deeds, in their conduct of the path of life, are upon insight (بصيرة) regarding their affair, and upon guidance (هدى) and mercy (رحمة) from their Lord, as He mentioned in the previous verse; whereas the evildoer is empty-handed of that. The Exalted said in another place: فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً — Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life (20:123-124). And He said in another place: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is that of one in manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge? (6:122).

As for their not being equal in death, it is because death—as dictated by radiant proofs—is not the annihilation of a thing or the nullification of the human soul as the followers of falsehood imagine. Rather, it is a return to Allah, the Glorified, and a transition from the realm of this world to the realm of the Hereafter, which is the abode of permanence (دار البقاء) and the world of eternity (عالم الخلود), wherein the righteous believer lives in felicity and blessing, while others live in misery and punishment.

The Glorified has alluded to this in His previous speech by His saying: كَذلِكَ يُحْيِ اللهُ الْمَوْتى — Thus does Allah give life to the dead — and His saying: ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ — then to your Lord you will be brought back — and other such verses. He will address it further with His saying: وَخَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ — Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth — etc.

The verse, in terms of its word structure and the resulting meaning, is a battlefield of opinions among the exegetes, and they have mentioned many interpretations for it. What the context (السياق) provides and supports is what we have presented, and there is not much benefit in addressing the other views they have mentioned; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the extensive commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: وَخَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth, so that every soul may be recompensed for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged (45:22). It appears that what is meant by "the heavens and the earth" is the totality of the witnessed world. The ba in "with the truth" (بِالْحقِ) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة); thus, the creation of the world "with the truth" means it is a reality (حق), not falsehood (باطل) or play, which implies that this world of generation and corruption has a fixed, permanent purpose (غاية) beyond it.

His saying: وَلِتُجْزى — so that [every soul] may be recompensed — etc., is coordinated with "with the truth." The ba in His saying "for what it has earned" (بِما كَسَبَتْ) is for transitivity or for correspondence; that is, to be recompensed in exchange for what it earned: if it was obedience, then reward (الثواب); and if it was disobedience, then punishment (العقاب). His saying: وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — and they will not be wronged — is a circumstantial clause (حال) for "every soul"; meaning, so that every soul may be recompensed for what it earned with justice.

Thus, the meaning of the verse boils down to something like: Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth and with justice. The creation being with truth necessitates that beyond this world there is another world in which beings remain eternally; and the creation being with justice necessitates that every soul be recompensed with what it deserves by its earning—so the doer of good is recompensed with a good reward, and the doer of evil is recompensed with an evil reward. Since this does not occur in this realm, it must be in another realm.

By this explanation, it becomes clear that the verse contains two arguments for the Return: one alluded to by His saying: "Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth," which proceeds by way of truth; and the second alluded to by His saying: "so that [every soul] may be recompensed," etc., which proceeds by way of justice.

The two arguments thus boil down to what is contained in His saying: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ — We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who disbelieve; so woe to the disbelievers from the Fire. Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious? (38:27-28).

The verse, with the argument it contains, nullifies their assumption that the evildoer is like the doer of good in death; for the matter of recompense with reward and punishment for obedience and disobedience on the Day of Resurrection negates the equality of the obedient and the disobedient in death. Consequently, this nullifies their assumption that the evildoer is like the doer of good in life; for the establishment of recompense on that day necessitates the obligation of obedience in this world. The doer of good is upon insight regarding the affair in his life, performing the required action and providing for his tomorrow from his today, unlike the evildoer who lives in blindness and misguidance; thus, they are not equal.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ — Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly (45:23) — until the end of the verse. The context suggests that His saying "Have you seen" (أَفَرَأَيْتَ) is intended to evoke wonder; meaning: Are you not amazed by one whose state is such? What is meant by His saying "taken his desire to be his god," where He placed "his god" (إِلهَهُ) before "his desire" (هَواهُ), is that he knows he has a God Whom he must worship—namely Allah, the Glorified—but he replaces Him with his desire and puts his desire in His place, thus worshipping it. He is therefore a disbeliever in Allah, the Glorified, despite his knowledge. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: "and whom Allah has led astray knowingly" (وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ); meaning that he is astray from the path while he knows it.

The meaning of "taking as a god" is worship (العبادة), and what is meant by it is obedience (الإطاعة); for Allah, the Glorified, counted obedience as worship, as in His saying: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي — Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me” (36:60-61). And His saying: اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah (9:31). And His saying: وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — and that none of us shall take others for lords besides Allah (3:64).

Rational consideration (الاعتبار) agrees with this, for worship is nothing but the manifestation of submission and the representation that the worshipper is a servant who neither wills nor acts except what his object of worship wills and is pleased with. Thus, whoever obeys a thing has taken it as a god and worshipped it. Whoever obeys his desire has taken his desire as his god; and there is no obedience except to Allah or to one whose obedience He has commanded.

So His saying: "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god" means: Are you not amazed by one who worships his desire by obeying and following it, while he knows he has a God other than it Whom he must worship and obey, yet he makes his object of worship and obedience his desire?

His saying: وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ — and whom Allah has led astray knowingly — meaning he is astray by a misguidance from the Exalted, with which He misleads him as a recompense for his following of desire, while his misguidance is established upon the knowledge of this astray person. There is no harm in the coexistence of misguidance with knowledge of the path and recognition of it, as in the saying of the Exalted: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ — They impugned them—though they were convinced in their hearts (14:14). This is because knowledge does not necessitate guidance, nor does misguidance necessitate ignorance. Rather, what necessitates guidance is knowledge accompanied by the knower's commitment (التزام) to the requirements of his knowledge; then guidance follows. But if the knower does not commit to the requirements of his knowledge due to following desire, there is no cause for his guidance; rather, it is misguidance, even if knowledge is present.

As for the saying of some that what is meant by "knowledge" is the knowledge of the Exalted—meaning: "and Allah led him astray based on His knowledge of his state"—it is far-fetched from the context.

His saying: وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً — and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight — is like an explanatory coordination for His saying: "and whom Allah has led astray knowingly." The "sealing" (الختم) upon the hearing and the heart means that he neither hears the truth nor understands it. Placing the "blindfold" (الغشاوة) on the sight means that he does not see the truth from the signs of Allah. The sum of it all is: that no effect results from the hearing, the heart, and the sight—which is the commitment to the requirements of the truth he has attained when he perceives it—due to the arrogance of his soul and his following of desire. You have already learned that being astray from the path does not contradict knowing it if there is no commitment to its requirements.

His saying: فَمَنْ يَهْدِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللهِ — So who will guide him after Allah? — the pronoun refers to the one who took his desire as his god. The derivation (التفريع) is based on what has resulted from his state; meaning: if his state is such, and Allah has led him astray knowingly, etc., then who can guide him besides Allah, the Glorified? For there is no guide other than Him. The Exalted said: قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللهِ هُوَ الْهُدى — Say, “Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance” (2:120). And He said: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ — and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide (40:33).

His saying: أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ — Will you not then take admonition? — meaning: Will you not reflect on his state and remember that these people have no way to guidance while following desire, so that you may take heed?

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيا وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ — They say, “There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing destroys us but time” (45:24) — until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: Time (الدهر) is originally a name for the duration of the world from the beginning of its existence to its end; based on this is the saying of the Exalted: هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ — Has there been a period of time for man... (76:1). Then it is used to express any long duration, and it is the opposite of "time" (الزمان) in that zaman applies to both short and long durations. [End quote].

The verse—according to what the context provides, which is the context of arguing against the polytheists who affirm the Creator but deny the Return—is a narration of the speech of the polytheists in their denial of the Return, not the speech of the materialists (الدهريين) who attribute events, in existence and non-existence, to time and deny both the Origin and the Return altogether, as they were not mentioned in the previous verses.

So their saying: "There is nothing but the life of this world" (ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا)—the pronoun refers to life; meaning: there is no life for us except our worldly life, no life beyond it. Thus, there is no existence for what the divine religion claims regarding the Resurrection and the life of the Hereafter. This is the supporting evidence for the view that what is meant by His saying: "we die and we live" (نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيا) is: some of us die and others of us live, whereby the survival of the human race continues through the death of the predecessors and the life of the successors. This is somewhat supported by His saying afterward: "and nothing destroys us but time" (وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ), which suggests continuity.

The meaning is: The polytheists said: Life is nothing but our worldly life in which we live in this world; some of us—the predecessors—continue to die, and others—the successors—live, and nothing destroys us but time—which, by its passing, wears out everything new, corrupts every being, and kills every living thing. Thus, death is not a transition from one abode to another ending in Resurrection and the return to Allah.

Perhaps this is the speech of some of the ignorant among the Arab polytheists; otherwise, the doctrine prevalent among polytheists is reincarnation (التناسخ) — the belief that souls of those who have not reached perfection, upon departing their bodies, attach to other new bodies. If the departing soul had acquired felicity in its previous body, it attaches to a new body in which it enjoys bliss and happiness; and if it had acquired misery in the previous body, it attaches to a subsequent body in which it suffers and is punished as a recompense for its evil deed, and so on. These people do not deny the attribution of the matter of death, like life, to the mediation of angels.

For this reason—meaning the prevalence of the doctrine of reincarnation among polytheists—some exegetes mentioned that what is meant by the verse is their belief in reincarnation. The meaning would be: "There is nothing but the life of this world," so we never leave the world; "we die" from one worldly life "and we live" after death by attaching to a new body, and so on; "and nothing destroys us but time."

This is not without merit, but it is not well-suited to their speech quoted at the end: "and nothing destroys us but time," unless it is interpreted that their meaning in attributing destruction to time is that time is a means used by the angel in charge of death to cause death. Likewise, it is not well-suited to their argument quoted at the end: "Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful," which clearly shows that they see their fathers as non-existent, their essences nullified.

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse that are not significant, such as the saying of some: The meaning is that we were dead without life—which is before the breathing of the spirit—then we live by its breathing, similar to the saying of the Exalted: وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ — you were lifeless and He gave you life (2:28).

And the saying of some: What is meant by life is the survival of the lineage metaphorically; the meaning being: we die and we live through the survival of our lineage. Among other things that have been said.

His saying: وَما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ — They have no knowledge of that: they only conjecture — meaning that their saying, which implies the denial of the Return, is a speech without knowledge; it is merely a conjecture they hold. This is because they have no proof to indicate the negation of the Return, despite the presence of proofs for its establishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ ما كانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا ائْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — When Our manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument is to say, “Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful” (45:25). This is a confirmation that their speech negating the Return and restricting life to the worldly life is a speech without knowledge.

What is meant by "manifest signs" (الآيات البينات) are the verses containing the arguments proving the Return; their being "manifest" refers to the clarity of their indication of its establishment without doubt. Calling their saying: "Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful" an "argument" (حجة), despite it being a random demand after the establishment of proof, is by way of sarcasm (التهكم); for it is like requesting a proof for the objective after the proof for it has already been established. It is as if it were said: Their only argument was the non-argument.
