The meaning is: When Our verses containing the arguments proving the Return are recited to these deniers of the Return—while they are clear in their indication of its establishment—they do not confront them except with a random speech, which is the demand for proof of its possibility by reviving their deceased fathers.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — Say, “It is Allah who gives you life, then He makes you die, then He will gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. But most people do not know” (45:26) — until His saying — وَالْأَرْضِ — and the earth. What was mentioned of their demand for a proof for an objective for which proof had already been established—even if it was a random demand that does not require a response—nevertheless, the Glorified commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to answer them by affirming its possibility, which they considered far-fetched.

The gist of it is: The One Who gives you life for the first time, then makes you die, then gathers you to the Day of Resurrection in which there is no doubt, is Allah, the Glorified. To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth; He judges therein as He wills and disposes therein however He intends. Thus, it is for Him to judge the return of people to Him and to dispose of you by gathering you to the Day of Resurrection, and the judgment between you, then the recompense. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ الْمُبْطِلُونَ — And on the day when the Hour sets in, on that day the followers of falsehood will be the losers (45:27). Al-Raghib said: Loss (الخسر) and losing (الخسران) is the reduction of capital. This is attributed to the person, so it is said: "So-and-so lost"; and to the action, so it is said: "his trade lost." The Exalted said: تِلْكَ إِذاً كَرَّةٌ خاسِرَةٌ — That, then, would be a losing return (79:12). This is used for external acquisitions like wealth and status in the world—which is most common—and for psychological acquisitions like health, safety, intellect, faith, and reward—which is what Allah, the Exalted, made the "manifest loss" (الخسران المبين).

He said: Every loss mentioned by Allah, the Exalted, in the Quran is according to this latter meaning, not the loss related to financial acquisitions and human trades.

He said: Nullification (الإبطال) is said regarding the corruption of a thing and its removal, whether that thing is true or false. The Exalted said: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ — that He might confirm the truth and nullify falsehood (8:8). It may also be said of one who says something that has no reality, such as: وَلَئِنْ جِئْتَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ لَيَقُولَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُبْطِلُونَ — And if you bring them a sign, the disbelievers will surely say, “You are nothing but followers of falsehood” (30:58). And the saying of the Exalted: خَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ — there the followers of falsehood were the losers (40:78); meaning those who nullify the truth. [End quote].

It is most likely that what is meant by the "setting in of the Hour" (قيام الساعة) is the actualization of what occurs therein of resurrection, gathering, reckoning, and recompense, and its manifestation. Thereby, it is correct to make the Hour the vessel for the "day," and they are one. It is also most likely that His saying "on that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ) is a confirmation of His saying "on the day when the Hour sets in."

The meaning is: And on the day when the Hour sets in—which is the day of the return to Allah—on that day the followers of falsehood (المبطلون), who nullified the truth and turned away from it, will be the losers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرى كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جاثِيَةً كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا — And you will see every nation kneeling. Every nation will be summoned to its book (45:28), etc. Kneeling (الجثو) is sitting on the knees, just as al-jadhw is sitting on the tips of the toes.

The address is general to everyone from whom seeing is possible, even if it is directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). What is meant by the "summons to the book" is the summons to the reckoning according to what the book dictates by its enumeration of deeds, as evidenced by His saying afterward: الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — Today you will be recompensed for what you used to do.

The meaning is: You and other observers will see every nation among the nations sitting on their knees—the sitting of the humble and the fearful—every nation among them being summoned to its specific book, which is the record of deeds; and it is said to them: "Today you will be recompensed for what you used to do."

It is inferred from the literal meaning of the verse that every nation has a specific book for them, just as every human being has a specific book for him. The Exalted said: وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً — We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open (17:13).

His saying, the Exalted: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth. Indeed We used to record what you used to do (45:29). It is said in al-Sihah: "I copied the book" (نسخت الكتاب), "I transcribed it" (انتسخته), and "I had it transcribed" (استنسخته) all have the same meaning; and the "copy" (النسخة) is the name of that from which it is transcribed. [End quote]. Al-Raghib said: Transcription (النسخ) is the removal of a thing by something that follows it, like the sun removing the shade, the shade removing the sun, and gray hair removing youth—until he said—and copying a book is transferring its mere form to another book, and that does not necessitate the removal of the first form; rather, it necessitates the establishment of its like in another medium, like taking the impression of a signet ring in many waxes. Al-istinsakh is the request to copy a thing and the preparation for copying. [End quote].

What follows from what has been quoted is that the object to which the verb is transitive in our saying "I copied the book" is the original from which it is transferred. This necessitates that the deeds in His saying: "Indeed We used to record (نَسْتَنْسِخُ) what you used to do" be a book and an original—or if you wish, say: in an original and a book that is copied and transferred from. If the intention were to record the external deeds performed by the human being by writing them down, it would have been said: "Indeed We used to write (نكتب) what you used to do," as there is no subtle point (نكتة) that necessitates assuming these deeds to be a book and an original that is copied. There is no evidence for "copying" (يستنسخ) being in the sense of "writing down" (يستكتب) as some have mentioned.

This necessitates that what is meant by "what you do" is their external deeds insofar as they are in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ); thus, the copying of deeds is the copying of what relates to their deeds from the Preserved Tablet. The record of deeds would then be a record of deeds and a part of the Preserved Tablet; and the meaning of the angels' writing of the deeds would be their matching what they have of the copy of the Tablet with the deeds.

This is the meaning that has been narrated in reports from the paths of the Shi'a from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) and from the paths of the Sunnis from Ibn Abbas, and it will come to you in the following traditional discussion.

Based on this, His saying: "This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth" is from the speech of the Exalted, not from the speech of the angels. It is from His address to the people of the gathering on the Day of Resurrection, which He narrates to us; so it is in the sense of: "And it is said to them: This is Our book," etc.

The demonstration "this" (هذا)—according to what the context provides—refers to the record of deeds, which is itself a reference to the Preserved Tablet as previously mentioned. The attribution of the book to the Exalted is in view of it being the record of deeds from the perspective that it is written by His command, and in view of it being the Preserved Tablet from the perspective of honor. His saying: "speaks against you with the truth" (يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِ) means it bears witness to what you did and points to it with a clear indication accompanied by the truth.

His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — Indeed We used to record what you used to do — is a justification for the book speaking against them with the truth; meaning that this book of Ours points to your action with the truth without deviating from it, because it is the Preserved Tablet that encompasses your deeds in all their real aspects.

Were it not that the book shows them their deeds in a manner that no doubt enters it and no denial is possible from them, they would have denied it. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَها وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَداً بَعِيداً — The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done, it will wish there were a great distance between it and that [day] (3:30).

The scholars have other opinions regarding the verse:

Among them is what was said: that the verse is from the speech of the angels, not from the speech of Allah, and the meaning of istinsakh is writing. The meaning would be: This—meaning the record of deeds—is our book, the assembly of angels who write the deeds, bearing witness against you with the truth; indeed, we used to write what you used to do.

The problem with this is that its being from the speech of the angels is far-fetched from the context, besides the fact that istinsakh being in the sense of absolute writing is not established linguistically.

Among them: that the verse is from the speech of Allah, and the demonstration "this" refers to the record of deeds—and it was said: to the Preserved Tablet—and istinsakh is in the sense of requesting to write (استكتاب) absolutely.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ — As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest success (45:30). This is a detailing of the state of people on that day according to their differences in felicity and misery, reward and punishment. The felicitous who are rewarded are those who have faith and do righteous deeds; and the miserable who are punished are those who disbelieved from among the arrogant criminals.

What is meant by mercy (الرحمة) is the divine outpouring that brings felicity to whoever is established within it, and among it is Paradise. The manifest success (الفوز المبين) is the clear prosperity. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ آياتِي تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ — But as for those who disbelieve [they will be told]: “Were not My signs recited to you? But you were arrogant and you were a guilty lot” (45:31). What is meant by "those who disbelieve" are those characterized by disbelief out of denial and rejection, as evidenced by His saying: "Were not My signs recited to you? But you were arrogant," etc.

The fa in "Were not" (أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ) is for derivation (التفريع), indicating a suppressed clause upon which it is derived, which is the answer to "as for" (لما). The estimation is: "Then it is said to them: Were not My signs recited to you?" What is meant by the "signs" are the divine arguments delivered to them through revelation and invitation. The criminal (المجرم) is the one characterized by crime (الأجرام), which is sin.

The meaning is: As for those who disbelieved, rejecting the truth despite its manifestation, it is said to them by way of rebuke and reprimand: Were not My arguments read and explained to you in the world, but you were too arrogant to accept them and were a sinful people?

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَالسَّاعَةُ لا رَيْبَ فِيها قُلْتُمْ ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ — When it was said, “Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,” you said, “We do not know what the Hour is” (45:32), etc. What is meant by the "promise" (الوعد) is the promised thing (الموعود), which is what Allah promised by the tongues of His messengers regarding the Resurrection and recompense; thus, His saying: "and there is no doubt about the Hour" is an explanatory coordination. It is also possible that "promise" is intended in its infinitive sense.

Their saying: "We do not know what the Hour is" (ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ) means that it is not understood by them, while they are people of understanding and discernment; so it is a metaphor for it being an irrational matter—for if it were rational, they would have known it.

His saying: إِنْ نَظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَما نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ — “We only conjecture, and we are not certain” — meaning it is not something we are sure of or certain about; rather, we hold a conjecture that we cannot rely upon. Thus, in their saying: "We do not know what the Hour is," etc., following the recitation of the manifest signs to them, is the most heinous obstinacy against the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride (45:33). The addition of "evils" (سيئات) to "what they had done" (ما عَمِلُوا) is explanatory or in the sense of "from." What is meant by "what they had done" is the genus of what they did; meaning: their evil deeds appeared to them, or the evils from their deeds. Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ — The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done (3:30).

The verse is among the verses indicating the embodiment of deeds (تمثل الأعمال). It was said that there is an omission in the speech, the estimation being: "And the recompense for the evils of what they had done appeared to them."

His saying: وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — and they will be besieged by what they used to deride — meaning the punishment they used to mock in the world when they were warned of it by the tongues of the prophets and messengers descended upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنْساكُمْ كَما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا وَمَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ — It will be said, “Today We will forget you just as you forgot the meeting of this day of yours. Your abode will be the Fire, and you will have no helpers” (45:34). Forgetting (النسيان) is a metaphor for turning away and abandoning. Thus, the forgetting of them by the Exalted on the Day of Resurrection is His turning away from them and His abandoning them in its hardships and terrors; and their forgetting the meeting of that day of theirs in the world was their turning away from remembering it and their abandoning the preparation for meeting it. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّكُمُ اتَّخَذْتُمْ آياتِ اللهِ هُزُواً وَغَرَّتْكُمُ الْحياةُ الدُّنْيا — “That is because you took the signs of Allah in derision, and the life of the world deceived you” (45:35), etc. The demonstration "that" (ذلِكُمْ) refers to what was mentioned of their punishment—the appearance of evil deeds and the descent of punishment. Derision (الهزء) is the mockery that is made, and the ba denotes causality.

The meaning is: That punishment which descends upon you is because you took the signs of Allah as a mockery that you derided, and because the life of the world deceived you, so you clung to it and became attached to it.

His saying: فَالْيَوْمَ لا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْها وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ — So today they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to satisfy [Allah] — the address is shifted from them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The speech contains the summary of what will befall them of punishment on that day, which is eternity in the Fire and the non-acceptance of excuses from them.

Seeking to satisfy (الاستعتاب) is the request for favor and making amends; the negation of being asked to satisfy is a metaphor for the non-acceptance of excuses.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَرَبِّ الْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — So all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth, the Lord of all the worlds (45:36). This is a praise of Him, the Exalted, derived from what preceded in the Surah regarding His being the Creator of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, and the Disposer of the affairs of all. Among His wondrous disposal is the creation of all with truth, which entails the day of return to Him and the recompense for deeds; and this necessitates the establishment of Laws that lead to felicity and reward, followed by the gathering for the Day of Gathering, then the recompense and the establishment of all upon mercy and justice by giving everything what it deserves. Thus, He has not disposed except with a beautiful disposal, and He has not acted except with a praised act; so to Him belongs all praise.

He repeated "the Lord" (الرب), saying: رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَرَبِّ الْأَرْضِ (the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth), then substituted for them His saying: رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (the Lord of all the worlds), in order to explicitly state the encompassment of Lordship (الربوبية) over all. For if "the Lord of all the worlds" had been brought and sufficed with, it might have been imagined that He is the Lord of the collective, but for the heavens specifically there is another lord, and for the earth alone another lord, as the polytheists sometimes claimed. Likewise, if He had sufficed with "the heavens and the earth," it would not have been explicit in His Lordship over other than them; and similarly if He had sufficed with one of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise (45:37). Majesty (الكبرياء), according to what is from al-Raghib, is: rising above submission. From Ibn al-Athir: greatness and sovereignty. In al-Majma': the subduing authority, the subduing greatness, and grandeur and loftiness.

In any case, it is more profound in meaning than "greatness" (الكبر) and is used for non-sensory greatness; its reference is to the perfection of His existence and the infinity of His perfection.

His saying: وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth — meaning to Him belongs majesty in every place; nothing therein can exalt itself over Him, nor can anything belittle Him. The advancement of the predicate in "To Him belongs all majesty" (وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ) denotes exclusivity, as in His saying: "So all praise belongs to Allah" (فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ).

His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise — meaning the Victorious who is never defeated in what He intends of creation and disposal in this world and the Hereafter, and the One who builds His creation and disposal upon wisdom and precision.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ — Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god — he said: "It was revealed concerning the Quraysh; whenever they desired something, they worshipped it."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "A man from the Arabs used to worship a stone—then if he saw one better than it, he would take it and cast away the other—so Allah revealed: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ."

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ — and nothing destroys us but time — it has been narrated in a hadith from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: لا تسبوا الدهر فإن الله هو الدهر — "Do not revile time, for indeed Allah is time."

I say: Al-Tabarsi said after citing the hadith: "Its interpretation is that the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) used to attribute the devastating events and the descending calamities to time, saying: 'Time did such-and-such,' and they used to revile time. So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the Doer of these matters is Allah, so do not revile their Doer.'" [End quote]. This view is supported by the following narration.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma' wa al-Sifat narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: Let not the son of Adam say, reviling time: "O disappointment of time!" for I am time; I send the night and the day, and if I wish, I withdraw them.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِ — This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth — the verse: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr from Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "I asked him about ن وَالْقَلَمِ (Nun. By the Pen). He said: 'Indeed, Allah created the Pen from a tree in Paradise called al-Khuld (Eternity)—then He said to a river in Paradise: "Be ink," and the river solidified—and it was whiter than snow and sweeter than honey. Then He said to the Pen: "Write." It said: "O Lord, what shall I write?" He said: "Write what was and what is to be until the Day of Resurrection"—so the Pen wrote on a parchment whiter than silver—and clearer than ruby. Then He folded it and placed it in a pillar of the Throne, then He sealed the mouth of the Pen—so it shall never speak again.'

'It is the Hidden Book (الكتاب المكنون) from which all copies (النسخ) are made—are you not Arabs? How do you not know the meaning of the speech? One of you says to his companion: "Copy (انسخ) that book"—is it not that he only copies from another book, from the original? And that is His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (Indeed We used to record what you used to do).'"

I say: His saying (عليه‌السلام): "so the Pen wrote on a parchment," etc., is a representation of the Tablet in which events are written as a parchment (الرق); and the parchment is that upon which one writes, similar to paper—as mentioned by al-Raghib. The hadith has already preceded from him (عليه‌السلام) that the Pen is an angel and the Tablet is an angel. His saying: "placed it in a pillar of the Throne" is a representation of the Throne as the throne of a king having pillars and legs. His saying: "then He sealed the mouth of the Pen," etc., is a metaphor for what was written in the parchment being a decreed judgment (قضاء محتوم) that neither changes nor is altered. His saying: "are you not Arabs," etc., is an allusion to what was previously clarified in the commentary of the verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Indeed, Allah created the Nun, which is the inkwell, and He created the Pen—then He said: 'Write.' It said: 'What shall I write?' He said: 'Write what is to be until the Day of Resurrection of deeds performed—righteous or wicked—or provision provided—lawful or prohibited—then He attached to everything its state: its entry into the world and its stay therein for how long, and its exit from it how?'

'Then He placed over the servants guardians (حفظة) and over the Book treasurers who guard it—they copy (ينسخون) every day from the treasurers the work of that day—so when that provision is exhausted, the matter is cut off and the term is ended—the guardians come to the treasurers seeking the work of that day—the treasurers say to them: "We find nothing for your companion with us"—so the guardians return and find that they have died.'

Ibn Abbas said: 'Are you not an Arab people? Do you not hear the guardians saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (Indeed We used to record what you used to do)? And can copying (الاستنساخ) be from anything but an original?'"

I say: The report, as you see, makes the verse from the speech of the guardian angels.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: regarding the verse, he said: "The guardians copy from the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب)—what the children of Adam do—for the human being only acts according to what the angel has copied from the Mother of the Book."

And from the book Sa'd al-Su'ud by Ibn Tawus, he said after mentioning the two angels entrusted with the servant: "In a narration: When they intend to descend morning and evening—Israfil copies for them the deed of the servant from the Preserved Tablet—so he gives them that. Then when they ascend morning and evening with the register of the servant—Israfil compares it with the copies he had transcribed for them—until it becomes apparent that it was exactly as was copied from it."

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth — and in the hadith, Allah says: الكبرياء ردائي والعظمة إزاري ـ فمن نازعني واحدة منهما ألقيته في نار جهنم — "Majesty is My cloak and greatness is My lower garment—so whoever disputes with Me for either of them, I shall cast him into the fire of Hell."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur by Muslim, Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, and others from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).
